source_entries.lesson: Enemies that turrets or stationary shooters placed on wide horizontal platforms become harmless if the player can pass beneath their firing arc — elevation or vertical offset is required for threat relevance.
Turrets or stationary shooters placed on wide horizontal platforms become harmless if the player can pass beneath their firing arc — elevation or vertical offset is required for threat relevance.

Stationary shooters placed on the same elevation as the player's movement path become irrelevant if the player can pass beneath their firing line — vertical offset or elevation change is required to make them threatening.
Repetitive enemy patterns without variation in positioning or timing create predictable, low-engagement encounters even with high enemy counts.
Repeating identical platform-and-turret patterns without variation in spacing, height, or timing create predictable, low-engagement encounters that players optimize away.
A single repeated macro-pattern (e.g., stair-step hills) across an entire level creates visual and mechanical fatigue even if local geometry varies slightly.
A level composed of a single repeated macro-structure (e.g., one continuous slope) creates mechanical fatigue even if local geometry varies slightly.
A level with clean platforming but zero enemies or hazards can still feel flat and low-fun even with a perfect, fast clear — traversal alone isn't enough stimulation.
Fast completion time with zero deaths/falls signals the level was too easy/under-stimulating rather than well-tuned.
A level that can be beaten by holding one direction with zero player inputs beyond movement provides no engagement and will always rate poorly regardless of polish.
Exit placement at the same elevation as spawn with no intervening obstacles turns the level into a walk simulator rather than a platformer.
Enemies and hazard density can make a level feel fun and engaging even when the layout has no vertical variation — fun and 'verticality' are judged as separate axes by players.
Flat levels with no vertical traversal (climbing/descending) and poorly positioned aerial enemies fail to deliver engaging platforming or combat.
Enemy variety without terrain variety still yields low engagement — enemies need diverse spatial contexts (verticality, cover, chokepoints) to create distinct encounters.
A level composed of a single repeated macro-structure (e.g., stair-step hills or one continuous slope) creates mechanical fatigue even if local geometry varies slightly.

merged_from.lesson: A level composed of a single repeated macro-structure (e.g., one continuous slope) creates mechanical fatigue even if local geometry varies slightly.

source_entries.lesson: Terrain variance requires distinct spatial contexts — gaps, platforms at multiple elevations, ceilings, and chokepoints — not just a continuous height gradient.

Horizontal length alone cannot substitute for structural variety — when a level extends for hundreds of tiles without meaningful changes in elevation, pacing, or encounter type, players perceive it as a grind rather than a progression.
conditions: level width greatly exceeds height
no significant elevation changes over long stretches
repeating enemy/platform patterns
source_entries.outcome: player disengagement, mid-level falls from fatigue, incomplete run, mediocre rating despite functional mechanics

source_entries.lesson: When a single jump archetype (e.g., a fixed-height gap or climb) repeats at high density across a long horizontal stretch, it creates mechanical fatigue that manifests as late-level falls and disengagement, even if the jump is fair in isolation.
conditions: level width greatly exceeds height
one jump type dominates traversal
repetition density is high over hundreds of tiles
source_entries.outcome: player falls from fatigue deep in level, rates experience poorly despite functional mechanics

source_entries.lesson: A level that spans hundreds of tiles horizontally but offers no way to climb or descend forces the player into a one-dimensional grind; even fair jumps and enemies cannot prevent disengagement when elevation never changes.
conditions: level width greatly exceeds height
no ladders, climbable walls, or vertical traversal mechanics
player spawns at bottom with exit at similar elevation
source_entries.outcome: player falls into unrecoverable pits deep in the level, spends 11+ minutes without completing, rates experience 2/5

source_entries.lesson: Long uninterrupted ladder climbs without rest platforms or horizontal traversal options cause player fatigue and falls, turning vertical traversal into a punishment loop rather than a challenge.
conditions: vertical shaft exceeds ~10 tiles height
ladders are the sole traversal method
no horizontal ledges, platforms, or branching paths along the climb
source_entries.outcome: player falls mid-climb, loses significant progress, disengages from vertical section

source_entries.lesson: Ladder climbs that exceed 8 tiles in height and lack horizontal ledges, rest platforms, or alternative paths become fatigue traps — players fall repeatedly at the same height, lose all progress, and perceive the climb as unfair rather than skillful.
conditions: ladder height > 8 tiles
no horizontal ledges or rest platforms on climb
no alternative traversal paths (wall-kick, moving platforms, etc.)
fall locations cluster at same ladder x-position
source_entries.outcome: player falls repeatedly at same ladder, respawns to start, repeats climb 5+ times without success

source_entries.lesson: When a level's horizontal span exceeds 200 tiles without mid-level checkpoints or safe zones, any fall in the latter half forces players to re-traverse 5+ minutes of content, converting challenge into tedium and causing disengagement.
conditions: level width > 200 tiles
no mid-level checkpoints or safe zones
falls occur in second half of level
respawn returns player to start
source_entries.outcome: player repeats 100+ seconds of traversal after each late fall, eventually quitting without completing

source_entries.lesson: When a level's horizontal span exceeds 200 tiles without mid-level checkpoints, any fall in the latter half forces players to re-traverse 5+ minutes of content, converting challenge into tedium and causing disengagement.
conditions: level width > 200 tiles
no mid-level checkpoints or safe zones
falls occur in second half of level
respawn returns player to start
source_entries.outcome: player repeats 100+ seconds of traversal after each late fall, eventually quitting without completing

source_entries.lesson: A level that can be completed quickly with minimal player input (holding one direction, zero deaths/falls, no obstacles or enemies) fails to engage players regardless of polish — traversal alone without meaningful interaction is insufficient for fun.
conditions: level can be beaten by holding one direction
zero enemies or hazards present
exit at same elevation as spawn with no intervening obstacles
fast completion time with zero deaths/falls
source_entries.outcome: players rate level poorly (1-3/5) despite functional mechanics; perceived as 'walk simulator' or 'flat' rather than a platformer

merged_from.lesson: Fast completion time with zero deaths/falls signals the level was too easy/under-stimulating rather than well-tuned.
A level that can be beaten by holding one direction with zero player inputs beyond movement provides no engagement and will always rate poorly regardless of polish.
Exit placement at the same elevation as spawn with no intervening obstacles turns the level into a walk simulator rather than a platformer.

source_entries.lesson: Deep, unclimbable pits at the bottom of a level act as soft locks — players perceive them as unfair traps rather than skill challenges when no recovery path exists.
conditions: pit depth exceeds max jump height
no wall-kick or climb surfaces
exit located above pit
source_entries.outcome: player falls, cannot recover, rates level poorly despite earlier engagement

source_entries.lesson: Enemy density and variety cannot compensate for flat terrain lacking vertical traversal — engaging combat requires diverse spatial contexts (elevation changes, cover, chokepoints) that flat levels cannot provide.
conditions: level has no vertical traversal (climbing/descending)
enemies present but placed on flat horizontal plane
aerial enemies positioned above player's projectile arc
source_entries.outcome: players rate level poorly (1-3/5); combat feels unfair or trivial, platforming feels absent; fun and verticality judged as separate axes

merged_from.lesson: Flat levels with no vertical traversal (climbing/descending) and poorly positioned aerial enemies fail to deliver engaging platforming or combat.
Enemy variety without terrain variety still yields low engagement — enemies need diverse spatial contexts (verticality, cover, chokepoints) to create distinct encounters.

source_entries.lesson: Long uninterrupted ladder climbs exceeding ~8-10 tiles without rest platforms, horizontal ledges, or alternative traversal paths cause player fatigue and repeated falls at the same height, turning vertical traversal into a punishment loop rather than a skill challenge.
conditions: ladder height exceeds ~8-10 tiles
ladders are the sole traversal method
no horizontal ledges, rest platforms, or branching paths along the climb
no alternative traversal paths (wall-kick, moving platforms, etc.)
vertical shaft exceeds ~10 tiles height
no horizontal ledges, platforms, or branching paths along the climb
ladder height > 8 tiles
no horizontal ledges or rest platforms on climb
fall locations cluster at same ladder x-position
source_entries.outcome: player falls repeatedly at same ladder height, loses significant progress, disengages from vertical section, perceives climb as unfair rather than skillful

merged_from.lesson: Ladder climbs that exceed 8 tiles in height and lack horizontal ledges, rest platforms, or alternative paths become fatigue traps — players fall repeatedly at the same height, lose all progress, and perceive the climb as unfair rather than skillful.

source_entries.lesson: Repeating identical enemy-and-platform patterns without variation in spacing, height, or timing creates predictable, low-engagement encounters that players optimize away.

merged_from.lesson: Repeating identical platform-and-turret patterns without variation in spacing, height, or timing create predictable, low-engagement encounters that players optimize away.

source_entries.lesson: Vertical challenge clusters near the level end amplify perceived difficulty spikes — players who traverse long horizontal stretches expect the finale to test mastery, not introduce new traversal demands without checkpoints.
conditions: long horizontal traversal precedes final vertical section
no mid-level checkpoints or safe zones before finale
final section requires repeated precise climbs
source_entries.outcome: player falls late, respawns to start, repeats 100+ seconds of content, rates level 3/5 despite enjoying variety

source_entries.lesson: Terrain variance requires distinct spatial contexts — gaps, platforms at multiple elevations, ceilings, and chokepoints — not just a continuous height gradient or single repeated macro-structure (e.g., stair-step hills, continuous slope), which creates mechanical fatigue even with local geometry variation.
conditions: level composed of single repeated macro-structure
no distinct spatial contexts (gaps, multi-elevation platforms, ceilings, chokepoints)
continuous height gradient without traversal variety
source_entries.outcome: mechanical fatigue, disengagement, poor ratings despite functional local geometry

merged_from.lesson: A level composed of a single repeated macro-structure (e.g., one continuous slope) creates mechanical fatigue even if local geometry varies slightly.
Terrain variance requires distinct spatial contexts — gaps, platforms at multiple elevations, ceilings, and chokepoints — not just a continuous height gradient.

source_entries.lesson: When a level's horizontal span exceeds 200 tiles without mid-level checkpoints or safe zones, any fall in the latter half forces players to re-traverse 5+ minutes of content, converting challenge into tedium and causing disengagement.
conditions: level width > 200 tiles
no mid-level checkpoints or safe zones
falls occur in second half of level
respawn returns player to start
source_entries.outcome: player repeats 100+ seconds of traversal after each late fall, eventually quitting without completing

merged_from.lesson: When a level's horizontal span exceeds 200 tiles without mid-level checkpoints, any fall in the latter half forces players to re-traverse 5+ minutes of content, converting challenge into tedium and causing disengagement.

source_entries.lesson: Persistent projectiles or hazards from dead enemies (e.g., stingers, bullets) that remain active after the enemy is killed create perceived unfairness, as players expect threat removal on kill.
conditions: enemy dies but its projectile/hazard remains active
player can be damaged by remnant hazard after kill
no visual cue that hazard persists
source_entries.outcome: player takes damage they feel is unavoidable and unjust, rates level poorly, calls out mechanic explicitly

source_entries.lesson: Vertical shafts built from repeating identical platform clusters without visual or spatial differentiation create navigation ambiguity — players cannot distinguish progress-critical paths from dead ends, leading to oscillation and perceived impossibility.
conditions: vertical shaft exceeds 10 tiles height
platform clusters repeat at uniform spacing
no landmarks, color cues, or structural breaks to mark the critical path
multiple enemy types placed without spatial hierarchy
source_entries.outcome: player oscillates vertically at same x-positions for 30+ seconds, fails to locate exit, rates level 1/5

source_entries.lesson: Repeating identical platform-enemy clusters at uniform horizontal intervals creates a predictable rhythm that players optimize away, eliminating engagement even when individual jumps or enemies are functional in isolation.
conditions: level width exceeds 100 tiles
platform pattern repeats at fixed spacing
enemy types and positions repeat per cluster
no elevation change or structural breaks between clusters
source_entries.outcome: player traverses quickly with minimal input variation, backtracks on flat sections, rates level 1/5 for repetition

source_entries.lesson: Horizontal length without structural variety (elevation changes, vertical traversal mechanics, varied encounter types) creates a one-dimensional grind that causes mechanical fatigue, late-level falls, and disengagement — even fair individual jumps/enemies cannot prevent this when repetition density is high over hundreds of tiles.
conditions: level width greatly exceeds height (hundreds of tiles)
no significant elevation changes over long stretches
no ladders, climbable walls, or vertical traversal mechanics
single jump archetype or encounter pattern repeats at high density
player spawns at bottom with exit at similar elevation
level width greatly exceeds height
repeating enemy/platform patterns
one jump type dominates traversal
repetition density is high over hundreds of tiles
source_entries.outcome: player falls from fatigue deep in level, spends 11+ minutes without completing, rates experience 1-2/5 despite functional mechanics in isolation

merged_from.lesson: When a single jump archetype (e.g., a fixed-height gap or climb) repeats at high density across a long horizontal stretch, it creates mechanical fatigue that manifests as late-level falls and disengagement, even if the jump is fair in isolation.

A level that spans hundreds of tiles horizontally but offers no way to climb or descend forces the player into a one-dimensional grind; even fair jumps and enemies cannot prevent disengagement when elevation never changes.
